title: Classification Metrics Implementation
description: Implement precision, recall, and F1 score from scratch using a confusion matrix.
starter_code:
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
import numpy as np

# Sample predictions and true labels
y_true = [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0]
y_pred = [0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]

# Calculate confusion matrix
tn, fp, fn, tp = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred).ravel()

# TODO: Calculate precision, recall, F1 from TP, TN, FP, FN
# Precision = TP / (TP + FP)
# Recall = TP / (TP + FN)
# F1 = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)

print(f"Precision: {precision}")
print(f"Recall: {recall}")
print(f"F1 Score: {f1}")
solution:
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
import numpy as np

# Sample predictions and true labels
y_true = [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0]
y_pred = [0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1]

# Calculate confusion matrix
tn, fp, fn, tp = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred).ravel()

# Calculate metrics from confusion matrix
precision = tp / (tp + fp) if (tp + fp) > 0 else 0
recall = tp / (tp + fn) if (tp + fn) > 0 else 0
f1 = 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall) if (precision + recall) > 0 else 0

print(f"Precision: {precision:.3f}")
print(f"Recall: {recall:.3f}")
print(f"F1 Score: {f1:.3f}")
print(f"Verify: precision_score={precision_score(y_true, y_pred):.3f}")
print(f"Verify: recall_score={recall_score(y_true, y_pred):.3f}")
print(f"Verify: f1_score={f1_score(y_true, y_pred):.3f}")
hints: Confusion matrix returns TN, FP, FN, TP in order when using .ravel()
Precision measures: of predicted positives, how many were correct
Recall measures: of actual positives, how many did we find
Check for division by zero

title: ROC Curve and AUC
description: Plot ROC curve and calculate AUC for a binary classifier.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc, roc_auc_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load data
data = load_breast_cancer()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(data.data, data.target, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Train model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# TODO: Get probability predictions and calculate ROC curve
# TODO: Calculate AUC score
# TODO: Plot ROC curve with AUC in legend

plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc, roc_auc_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load data
data = load_breast_cancer()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(data.data, data.target, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Train model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Get probability predictions
y_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# Calculate ROC curve
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_proba)
auc_score = auc(fpr, tpr)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, color='darkorange', lw=2, label=f'ROC curve (AUC = {auc_score:.3f})')
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color='navy', lw=2, linestyle='--', label='Random Classifier')
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

print(f"AUC Score: {auc_score:.3f}")
hints: Use predict_proba() to get probability predictions, take column 1
roc_curve returns fpr, tpr, thresholds in that order
AUC can be calculated from fpr and tpr using auc()
Diagonal line (0,0) to (1,1) represents random classifier

title: Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation
description: Compare regular k-fold with stratified k-fold on imbalanced data.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
import numpy as np

data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# TODO: Use regular KFold (n_splits=5)
# TODO: Use StratifiedKFold (n_splits=5)
# TODO: Train model on each fold and evaluate with F1 score
# TODO: Compare mean F1 scores
# TODO: Print class distribution in a few folds to show difference

print("Regular KFold F1 scores:", kfold_scores)
print("Stratified KFold F1 scores:", stratified_scores)
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
import numpy as np

data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000, random_state=42)

# Regular KFold
kfold = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
kfold_scores = []
for train_idx, test_idx in kfold.split(X):
    X_train, X_test = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]
    y_train, y_test = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    pred = model.predict(X_test)
    kfold_scores.append(f1_score(y_test, pred))

# Stratified KFold
stratified = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
stratified_scores = []
for train_idx, test_idx in stratified.split(X, y):
    X_train, X_test = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]
    y_train, y_test = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    pred = model.predict(X_test)
    stratified_scores.append(f1_score(y_test, pred))

print(f"Regular KFold F1 scores: {[f'{s:.3f}' for s in kfold_scores]}")
print(f"Regular KFold mean F1: {np.mean(kfold_scores):.3f}")
print(f"Stratified KFold F1 scores: {[f'{s:.3f}' for s in stratified_scores]}")
print(f"Stratified KFold mean F1: {np.mean(stratified_scores):.3f}")
hints: KFold.split(X) returns indices, StratifiedKFold.split(X, y) requires y
stratified_scores typically have lower variance across folds
For imbalanced data, stratified k-fold ensures each fold is representative

title: Permutation Feature Importance
description: Calculate feature importance using permutation method (model-agnostic).
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import pandas as pd

data = load_iris()
X = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
y = data.target

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Train model
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# TODO: Calculate permutation importance on test set
# TODO: Create DataFrame with feature names and importance scores
# TODO: Sort by importance descending
# TODO: Plot importance scores

print(importance_df)
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_iris()
X = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
y = data.target

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Train model
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Calculate permutation importance
perm_importance = permutation_importance(model, X_test, y_test, n_repeats=10, random_state=42)

# Create DataFrame
importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': X.columns,
    'importance': perm_importance.importances_mean,
    'std': perm_importance.importances_std
}).sort_values('importance', ascending=False)

print(importance_df)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.barh(importance_df['feature'], importance_df['importance'])
plt.xlabel('Permutation Importance')
plt.title('Feature Importance (Permutation Method)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
hints: permutation_importance(model, X_test, y_test) evaluates on test set
Returns object with importances_mean and importances_std
Model-agnostic: works with any model, any dataset
Lower importance = feature less important for prediction

title: Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)
description: Use RFE to select top N features from a dataset.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFE
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
import pandas as pd

data = load_breast_cancer()
X = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
y = data.target

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# TODO: Create SVM model
# TODO: Use RFE to select top 10 features
# TODO: Train model on selected features
# TODO: Evaluate accuracy on full feature set vs selected features
# TODO: Print selected feature names

print("Selected features:", selected_features)
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFE
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
import pandas as pd

data = load_breast_cancer()
X = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
y = data.target

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Create SVM model
model = SVC(kernel='linear', random_state=42)

# Use RFE to select top 10 features
rfe = RFE(model, n_features_to_select=10)
rfe.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Selected features
selected_features = X.columns[rfe.support_].tolist()
print("Selected features:", selected_features)

# Train on full features
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_full = model.predict(X_test)
acc_full = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_full)

# Train on selected features
X_train_selected = X_train[selected_features]
X_test_selected = X_test[selected_features]
model_selected = SVC(kernel='linear', random_state=42)
model_selected.fit(X_train_selected, y_train)
y_pred_selected = model_selected.predict(X_test_selected)
acc_selected = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_selected)

print(f"Accuracy with all {X.shape[1]} features: {acc_full:.3f}")
print(f"Accuracy with {len(selected_features)} selected features: {acc_selected:.3f}")
hints: RFE(model, n_features_to_select=n) - specify how many features to keep
rfe.support_ is boolean array indicating selected features
RFE considers feature interactions during elimination
Useful for reducing model complexity while maintaining accuracy

title: Model Calibration Curves
description: Plot calibration curves to assess if predicted probabilities match true probabilities.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_breast_cancer()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(data.data, data.target, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Uncalibrated model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# TODO: Calculate calibration curve (using calibration_curve function)
# TODO: Plot calibration curve
# TODO: Plot diagonal line (perfect calibration)
# TODO: Interpret results

plt.xlabel('Mean Predicted Probability')
plt.ylabel('True Probability')
plt.title('Calibration Curve')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve, CalibratedClassifierCV
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_breast_cancer()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(data.data, data.target, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Uncalibrated model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# Calculate calibration curve
prob_true, prob_pred = calibration_curve(y_test, y_proba, n_bins=10)

# Calibrated model
calibrated_model = CalibratedClassifierCV(model, method='sigmoid', cv=5)
calibrated_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_proba_cal = calibrated_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

prob_true_cal, prob_pred_cal = calibration_curve(y_test, y_proba_cal, n_bins=10)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.plot(prob_pred, prob_true, marker='o', label='Uncalibrated')
plt.plot(prob_pred_cal, prob_true_cal, marker='s', label='Calibrated')
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle='--', color='gray', label='Perfect Calibration')
plt.xlabel('Mean Predicted Probability')
plt.ylabel('True Probability')
plt.title('Calibration Curve')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
hints: calibration_curve(y_test, y_proba, n_bins=10) splits predictions into bins
Perfect calibration: points lie on diagonal (predicted = true probability)
CalibratedClassifierCV applies isotonic regression or sigmoid calibration
Calibration important for probability predictions (e.g., medical, fraud)

title: Learning Curves for Bias-Variance Analysis
description: Plot learning curves to diagnose underfitting vs overfitting.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve
from sklearn.svm import SVC
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_digits()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# TODO: Calculate learning curves for SVM
# TODO: Plot train and validation curves
# TODO: Interpret: gap indicates overfitting, both high indicates underfitting

plt.xlabel('Training Set Size')
plt.ylabel('Score')
plt.title('Learning Curve')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve
from sklearn.svm import SVC
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_digits()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# Calculate learning curves
model = SVC(kernel='rbf', random_state=42)
train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
    model, X, y, cv=5, 
    train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10),
    scoring='accuracy'
)

# Calculate mean and std
train_mean = np.mean(train_scores, axis=1)
train_std = np.std(train_scores, axis=1)
val_mean = np.mean(val_scores, axis=1)
val_std = np.std(val_scores, axis=1)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(train_sizes, train_mean, label='Training Score', color='blue')
plt.fill_between(train_sizes, train_mean - train_std, train_mean + train_std, alpha=0.2, color='blue')
plt.plot(train_sizes, val_mean, label='Validation Score', color='red')
plt.fill_between(train_sizes, val_mean - val_std, val_mean + val_std, alpha=0.2, color='red')
plt.xlabel('Training Set Size')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.title('Learning Curve')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

print(f"Final train score: {train_mean[-1]:.3f}")
print(f"Final val score: {val_mean[-1]:.3f}")
print(f"Gap (overfitting): {train_mean[-1] - val_mean[-1]:.3f}")
hints: learning_curve returns train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores
train_scores and val_scores have shape (n_iterations, n_splits)
Plot mean ± std to show confidence bands
Large gap = overfitting, both low = underfitting, converge = good fit

title: Imbalanced Data with Class Weights
description: Handle imbalanced classification using class weights and appropriate metrics.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, roc_auc_score, classification_report
import numpy as np

data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# Create imbalanced dataset (keep only 200 samples, mostly negative)
np.random.seed(42)
idx = np.random.choice(len(y), 200, replace=False)
X, y = X[idx], y[idx]
y = np.where(y == 1, 1, 0)  # Ensure binary

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42, stratify=y)

print(f"Class distribution in test: {np.bincount(y_test)}")

# TODO: Train model without class weights
# TODO: Train model with class_weight='balanced'
# TODO: Compare F1 and ROC AUC scores

print("\nWithout class weights:")
print(classification_report(y_test, pred1))
print("\nWith class weights:")
print(classification_report(y_test, pred2))
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, roc_auc_score, classification_report
import numpy as np

data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# Create imbalanced dataset
np.random.seed(42)
idx = np.random.choice(len(y), 200, replace=False)
X, y = X[idx], y[idx]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42, stratify=y)

print(f"Class distribution: {np.bincount(y_test)}")

# Without class weights
model1 = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
model1.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred1 = model1.predict(X_test)
y_proba1 = model1.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

f1_1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred1)
auc_1 = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba1)

# With class weights
model2 = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight='balanced', random_state=42)
model2.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred2 = model2.predict(X_test)
y_proba2 = model2.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

f1_2 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred2)
auc_2 = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba2)

print(f"\nWithout class weights: F1={f1_1:.3f}, AUC={auc_1:.3f}")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred1))

print(f"\nWith class weights: F1={f1_2:.3f}, AUC={auc_2:.3f}")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred2))
hints: class_weight='balanced' automatically adjusts weights inversely to class frequencies
Always use stratified split for imbalanced data
Evaluate with F1 and ROC AUC, not accuracy
Weighted models typically improve minority class recall

title: SMOTE for Imbalanced Data
description: Apply SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling) to handle imbalanced data.
starter_code:
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, classification_report
import numpy as np

data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# Create imbalanced dataset
np.random.seed(42)
idx = np.random.choice(len(y), 200, replace=False)
X, y = X[idx], y[idx]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42, stratify=y)

print(f"Original training distribution: {np.bincount(y_train)}")

# TODO: Apply SMOTE to training data only
# TODO: Train model on SMOTE-resampled data
# TODO: Compare with unbalanced model
# TODO: Remember: only apply SMOTE to training data, not test data

print(f"After SMOTE: {np.bincount(y_train_smote)}")
solution:
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, classification_report
import numpy as np

data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# Create imbalanced dataset
np.random.seed(42)
idx = np.random.choice(len(y), 200, replace=False)
X, y = X[idx], y[idx]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42, stratify=y)

print(f"Original training distribution: {np.bincount(y_train)}")

# Apply SMOTE (only on training data!)
smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_train_smote, y_train_smote = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

print(f"After SMOTE: {np.bincount(y_train_smote)}")

# Model without SMOTE
model1 = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
model1.fit(X_train, y_train)
f1_1 = f1_score(y_test, model1.predict(X_test))

# Model with SMOTE
model2 = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
model2.fit(X_train_smote, y_train_smote)
f1_2 = f1_score(y_test, model2.predict(X_test))

print(f"\nF1 without SMOTE: {f1_1:.3f}")
print(classification_report(y_test, model1.predict(X_test)))

print(f"\nF1 with SMOTE: {f1_2:.3f}")
print(classification_report(y_test, model2.predict(X_test)))
hints: SMOTE creates synthetic minority samples by interpolating between neighbors
Only apply SMOTE to training data, never to test data (prevents data leakage)
Use fit_resample(X_train, y_train) to get balanced data
Combined with class_weight='balanced' for best results

title: Threshold Tuning for Business Objectives
description: Adjust classification threshold to optimize for specific business metrics.
starter_code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve, f1_score
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_breast_cancer()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(data.data, data.target, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Train model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# TODO: Get precision-recall curve
# TODO: Find threshold that maximizes F1 score
# TODO: Plot precision, recall, and F1 vs threshold
# TODO: Show predictions at different thresholds

plt.xlabel('Threshold')
plt.ylabel('Score')
plt.title('Metrics vs Classification Threshold')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
solution:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve, f1_score, precision_score, recall_score
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = load_breast_cancer()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(data.data, data.target, test_size=0.3, random_state=42)

# Train model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=10000, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# Get precision-recall curve
precision, recall, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_proba)

# Calculate F1 for each threshold
f1_scores = 2 * (precision[:-1] * recall[:-1]) / (precision[:-1] + recall[:-1] + 1e-10)
best_idx = np.argmax(f1_scores)
best_threshold = thresholds[best_idx]
best_f1 = f1_scores[best_idx]

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(thresholds, precision[:-1], label='Precision', marker='.')
plt.plot(thresholds, recall[:-1], label='Recall', marker='.')
plt.plot(thresholds, f1_scores, label='F1', marker='.')
plt.axvline(best_threshold, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Best F1 at {best_threshold:.2f}')
plt.xlabel('Classification Threshold')
plt.ylabel('Score')
plt.title('Metrics vs Classification Threshold')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

print(f"Best threshold: {best_threshold:.3f}")
print(f"Best F1 score: {best_f1:.3f}")

# Apply custom threshold
y_pred_custom = (y_proba >= best_threshold).astype(int)
print(f"\nMetrics at custom threshold {best_threshold:.3f}:")
print(f"Precision: {precision_score(y_test, y_pred_custom):.3f}")
print(f"Recall: {recall_score(y_test, y_pred_custom):.3f}")
print(f"F1: {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_custom):.3f}")
hints: Default threshold is 0.5, but business objectives may require different values
Lower threshold → higher recall (catch more positives, more false positives)
Higher threshold → higher precision (fewer false positives, miss some positives)
precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_proba) returns (precision, recall, thresholds)
